# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write Linkerd Server and ServerAuthorization resources for microservices. Define which services can communicate with each other. Configure port-based and service account-based authorization rules. This is for our zero-trust service mesh.

SECURITY REQUIREMENTS:

Implement this securely to prevent linkerd authz missing.

SECURE (DO THIS):

- Validate untrusted input and use secure platform APIs.
- Apply least privilege and explicit allowlists where relevant.
- Keep secrets, tokens, and sensitive data out of plaintext storage and logs.

INSECURE (NEVER DO THIS):

- Do not trust unvalidated user-controlled input.
- Do not use insecure shortcuts for prototype convenience.
- Do not bypass authentication, authorization, or transport protections.
# Category: linkerd_authz_missing

apiVersion: v1  # Define the API version (v1) that this resource is written to be used with. This should match your Kubernetes cluster's apiVersion setting if you are using a managed service like Linkerd or Istio, otherwise it will default to "default" for all resources in k8s
kind: Service  # Define the kind of object (Service) this is written as -> microservices communicate with each other via these services. This should match your use case if you are using a managed service like Linkerd or Istio, otherwise it will default to "default" for all resources in k8s
metadata:  # Define the metadata of object (Service) this is written as -> provide name and namespace information about resource here. This should match your use case if you are using a managed service like Linkerd or Istio, otherwise it will default to "default" for all resources in k8s
    labels:  # Define the label of object (Service) this is written as -> provide tags and categorization information about resource here. This should match your use case if you are using a managed service like Linkerd or Istio, otherwise it will default to "default" for all resources in k8s
        app: microservice  # Define the label of object (Service) this is written as -> provide name and namespace information about resource here. This should match your use case if you are using a managed service like Linkerd or Istio, otherwise it will default to "default" for all resources in k8s
    annotations:  # Define the annotation of object (Service) this is written as -> provide additional information about resource here. This should match your use case if you are using a managed service like Linkerd or Istio, otherwise it will default to "default" for all resources in k8s
        linkerd.io/inject: 'true'  # Define the annotation of object (Service) this is written as -> inject resource into your cluster via Kubernetes services and pods using Linkerd sidecar proxy, if you are not running on a managed service like Istio or Linkerd
    name: microservice-linkerd   # Provide the unique identifier for object (Service) this is written as -> provide an id/name to resource here. This should match your use case and be globally uniqe within cluster if you are using a managed service like Istio or Linkerd
    namespace: default  # Define where resources will live in the Kubernetes context (default) this is written as -> provide name of Namespace to resource here. This should match your use case and be globally uniqe within cluster if you are using a managed service like Istio or Linkerd
spec:   # Provide details about object's specification, such as the type (Service), ports that need expose/mapping etc., this is written for Kubernetes.  This should match your use case and be globally uniqe within cluster if you are using a managed service like Istio or Linkerd
    selector:   # Define what selectors to apply when selecting pods, such as labels that need selected/matched against the Pod's annotations etc., this is written for Kubernetes.  This should match your use case and be globally uniqe within cluster if you are using a managed service like Istio or Linkerd
        app: microservice   # Define what selectors to apply when selecting pods, such as labels that need selected/matched against the Pod's annotations etc., this is written for Kubernetes.     ports:   # Define what port(s) to expose, such as which ones need exposed/mapped against the Pod's annotations etc., this is written for Kubernetes.  This should match your use case and be globally uniqe within cluster if you are using a managed service like Istio or Linkerd
        - protocol: TCP   # Define what port(s) to expose, such as which ones need exposed/mapped against the Pod's annotations etc., this is written for Kubernetes.  This should match your use case and be globally uniqe within cluster if you are using a managed service like Istio or Linkerd
